title: Angular — Core Concepts for Backend Developers

sections.heading: What is Angular? (and why TRAB uses it)
sections.content:
Angular is a TypeScript framework for building SPAs (Single Page Applications). TRAB uses Angular 19 for logistics interfaces — dashboards, planning tools, driver management. For a Java developer, Angular maps naturally:
Java/Spring conceptAngular equivalent
@Service (singleton bean)@Injectable() service
@Controller (REST endpoint)@Component (UI component)
Constructor injectioninject() function or constructor DI
Spring Security filterRoute Guard + HTTP Interceptor
@RequestMapping("/path")RouterModule routes
DTO (data transfer)TypeScript interface/type
Optional<T>T | null (union types)
Spring EventsSignals / RxJS Observables
Your honest position: "I built the SinapiPRO frontend with Angular 19, using Signals, standalone components, and PrimeNG. I understand the mental model — components, services, routing, interceptors. I'm not an Angular expert, but I can build functional interfaces and contribute to frontend tasks."

sections.heading: Components — the building blocks
Version context
sections.content: This content references Angular 19. Angular evolves rapidly. Key concepts (components, signals, standalone, DI) remain stable across versions. For interviews, understanding the architectural patterns matters more than specific version numbers. Post-Angular 19 evolution: • Angular 20+: further signal integration, improved SSR • The standalone-first approach and signal-based reactivity continue as the primary direction

sections.heading: Services — like Spring @Service
sections.content:
// delivery.service.ts
@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })  // singleton — like @Service
export class DeliveryService {
  private http = inject(HttpClient);
  private baseUrl = '/api/v1/deliveries';

  findAll(): Observable<Delivery[]> {
    return this.http.get<Delivery[]>(this.baseUrl);
  }

  findById(id: number): Observable<Delivery> {
    return this.http.get<Delivery>(`${this.baseUrl}/${id}`);
  }

  create(request: CreateDeliveryRequest): Observable<Delivery> {
    return this.http.post<Delivery>(this.baseUrl, request);
  }

  dispatch(id: number): Observable<Delivery> {
    return this.http.post<Delivery>(`${this.baseUrl}/${id}/dispatch`, {});
  }
}

// TypeScript interfaces (like Java DTOs)
interface Delivery {
  id: number;
  status: DeliveryStatus;
  driverName: string;
  scheduledAt: string;
  stops: Stop[];
}

type DeliveryStatus = 'PLANNED' | 'DISPATCHED' | 'IN_TRANSIT' | 'DELIVERED';
Observable vs Promise: • Observable (RxJS): lazy, cancellable, can emit multiple values (streams) • Used for HTTP calls, WebSocket, real-time data • .subscribe() triggers the HTTP call (lazy — nothing happens until subscribed)

sections.heading: Signals — modern reactive state (Angular 16+)
sections.content:
// Signals replace BehaviorSubject for component state
export class DeliveryDashboardComponent {
  // Writable signal (state)
  deliveries = signal<Delivery[]>([]);
  selectedDelivery = signal<Delivery | null>(null);

  // Computed signal (derived — like a computed property)
  activeCount = computed(() =>
    this.deliveries().filter(d => d.status === 'ACTIVE').length
  );

  delayedCount = computed(() =>
    this.deliveries().filter(d => d.status === 'DELAYED').length
  );

  // Update signal
  loadDeliveries() {
    this.deliveryService.findAll().subscribe(data => {
      this.deliveries.set(data);  // triggers re-render of anything using deliveries()
    });
  }

  selectDelivery(d: Delivery) {
    this.selectedDelivery.set(d);
  }
}

// In template:
// {{ activeCount() }} active deliveries
// {{ delayedCount() }} delayed
Signals vs RxJS: • Signals: simple synchronous state, auto-tracks dependencies, no memory leaks • RxJS: async streams, complex transformations, HTTP calls • Modern Angular: Signals for component state, RxJS for async operations

sections.heading: HTTP Interceptors — like Servlet Filters
sections.content:
// auth.interceptor.ts — injects JWT token into every request
export var authInterceptor: HttpInterceptorFn = (req, next) => {
  var token = inject(AuthService).getToken();

  if (token) {
    req = req.clone({
      setHeaders: { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}` }
    });
  }

  return next(req);
};

// error.interceptor.ts — handles 401/403 globally
export var errorInterceptor: HttpInterceptorFn = (req, next) => {
  return next(req).pipe(
    catchError(error => {
      if (error.status === 401) {
        inject(Router).navigate(['/login']);
      }
      return throwError(() => error);
    })
  );
};

// Register in app config:
provideHttpClient(withInterceptors([authInterceptor, errorInterceptor]))
Same pattern as Spring Security filters — intercept request, add auth header, handle errors globally.

sections.heading: Routing — like Spring @RequestMapping
sections.content:
// app.routes.ts
export var routes: Routes = [
  { path: '', redirectTo: '/dashboard', pathMatch: 'full' },
  { path: 'login', component: LoginComponent },
  {
    path: 'dashboard',
    component: DashboardComponent,
    canActivate: [authGuard]  // like Spring Security — must be authenticated
  },
  {
    path: 'deliveries',
    canActivate: [authGuard],
    children: [
      { path: '', component: DeliveryListComponent },
      { path: ':id', component: DeliveryDetailComponent },
      { path: ':id/edit', component: DeliveryEditComponent }
    ]
  },
  { path: '**', component: NotFoundComponent }  // 404
];

// Route guard (like @PreAuthorize)
export var authGuard: CanActivateFn = () => {
  var auth = inject(AuthService);
  var router = inject(Router);
  return auth.isAuthenticated() ? true : router.navigate(['/login']);
};

sections.heading: PrimeNG — like PrimeFaces for Angular
sections.content:
If you know PrimeFaces (JSF), you know PrimeNG:
PrimeFaces (Java EE)PrimeNG (Angular)
<p:dataTable><p-table>
<p:dialog><p-dialog>
<p:autoComplete><p-autoComplete>
<p:chart><p-chart> (or ECharts)
<p:calendar><p-datePicker>
<p:commandButton><p-button>
LazyDataModel[lazy]="true" + (onLazyLoad)
rendered="#{}"*ngIf / @if
value="#{bean.list}"[value]="deliveries()"
Interview answer: "I've used PrimeFaces extensively — PrimeNG is the same component library for Angular. The concepts transfer: data tables with lazy loading, dialogs, forms with validation, charts. The API is different but the mental model is identical."

sections.heading: Senior Perspective
sections.content:
Senior engineers understand Angular as an opinionated platform for enterprise applications, not just a component framework.

Key distinctions:
• Signals replace most uses of RxJS for component state — simpler mental model, better performance
• Change detection strategy (OnPush) is not optional for performance — it should be the default
• Lazy loading at the route level is the minimum — @defer provides component-level code splitting
• Standalone components eliminate NgModules — the mental model is simpler but requires explicit imports
• Testing Angular is about testing behavior through the component's public API, not implementation details

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content:
In a TRAB driver dashboard (Angular frontend):

• Real-time delivery updates via Server-Sent Events, rendered through signals
• Route visualization with lazy-loaded map component (@defer until visible)
• Offline capability for drivers in areas without connectivity (service worker + IndexedDB)
• PrimeNG data tables for warehouse managers with server-side pagination
• Strict Content Security Policy — no inline scripts, sanitized user input

sections.heading: When NOT to use
sections.content:
When Angular may not be the best choice:

• Simple marketing pages or blogs (static site generator is lighter)
• Small internal tools where React/Vue's lower ceremony speeds development
• When the team is primarily backend with limited frontend experience (consider HTMX or server-rendered)
• Real-time collaborative applications where frameworks with fine-grained reactivity (SolidJS) perform better
• Mobile-first applications where React Native or Flutter provide better native experience

sections.heading: RxJS — Reactive Extensions for JavaScript
sections.content:
RxJS is the async backbone of Angular. It provides Observables — lazy, cancellable streams of data. Java parallel:
JavaRxJS
CompletableFuture<T>Observable<T> (single emission)
Stream<T>Observable<T> (multiple emissions)
.thenApply()pipe(map())
.thenCompose()pipe(switchMap())
.exceptionally()pipe(catchError())
ExecutorServiceScheduler
Key difference: Observables are lazy — nothing executes until you .subscribe(). Like a Java Stream that doesn't run until a terminal operation.

sections.heading: Essential operators (the 20% that solves 80%)
sections.content:
// map — transform each emission (like Stream.map)
this.http.get<UserDTO[]>('/api/users').pipe(
  map(users => users.filter(u => u.active))
);

// switchMap — flatten + cancel previous (search autocomplete)
this.searchInput.valueChanges.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),
  distinctUntilChanged(),
  switchMap(term => this.http.get(`/api/search?q=${term}`))
);

// catchError — handle errors in the stream
this.http.get('/api/deliveries').pipe(
  catchError(err => {
    this.error.set('Failed to load deliveries');
    return of([]);  // fallback empty array
  })
);

// takeUntilDestroyed — auto-unsubscribe (Angular 16+)
private destroyRef = inject(DestroyRef);

ngOnInit() {
  this.ws.messages$.pipe(
    takeUntilDestroyed(this.destroyRef)
  ).subscribe(msg => this.messages.update(m => [...m, msg]));
}

// combineLatest — wait for multiple sources
combineLatest([this.drivers$, this.routes$]).pipe(
  map(([drivers, routes]) => this.assignDriversToRoutes(drivers, routes))
);
Operator decision guide: • Transform each value → map() • One Observable per emission (flatten) → switchMap() (cancel prev) or mergeMap() (parallel) • Side effects → tap() • Error handling → catchError() • Timing → debounceTime(), throttleTime() • Combine streams → combineLatest(), forkJoin()

sections.heading: Subjects — when you need to push values
sections.content:
// BehaviorSubject — holds current value (like a mutable signal)
@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class NotificationService {
  private notifications$ = new BehaviorSubject<Notification[]>([]);

  // Expose as Observable (read-only to consumers)
  readonly notifications = this.notifications$.asObservable();

  push(n: Notification) {
    this.notifications$.next([...this.notifications$.value, n]);
  }

  clear() {
    this.notifications$.next([]);
  }
}

// Subject types:
// Subject        — no initial value, hot (misses emissions before subscribe)
// BehaviorSubject — has initial value, replays latest to new subscribers
// ReplaySubject  — replays N past values to new subscribers
// AsyncSubject   — emits only the LAST value, on complete
Modern Angular preference: Use signal() for synchronous state, BehaviorSubject only when you need Observable integration (pipes, async operators).

sections.heading: Memory leaks — the #1 Angular pitfall
Signals — Angular's reactive primitive (Angular 16+)
Signal-based component pattern
sections.content:
@Component({
  selector: 'app-delivery-dashboard',
  standalone: true,
  changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush,
  template: `
    <h2>Dashboard ({{ totalActive() }} active)</h2>

    @if (loading()) {
      <p-progressSpinner />
    } @else {
      @for (d of filteredDeliveries(); track d.id) {
        <app-delivery-card [delivery]="d" (dispatched)="onDispatch($event)" />
      }
    }

    @if (filteredDeliveries().length === 0) {
      <p>No deliveries match filter.</p>
    }
  `
})
export class DeliveryDashboardComponent {
  private service = inject(DeliveryService);

  // State
  deliveries = signal<Delivery[]>([]);
  loading = signal(true);
  filter = signal<DeliveryStatus | 'ALL'>('ALL');

  // Derived
  filteredDeliveries = computed(() => {
    const f = this.filter();
    return f === 'ALL'
      ? this.deliveries()
      : this.deliveries().filter(d => d.status === f);
  });

  totalActive = computed(() =>
    this.deliveries().filter(d => d.status !== 'DELIVERED').length
  );

  constructor() {
    this.loadData();
  }

  private loadData() {
    this.service.findAll().subscribe({
      next: data => { this.deliveries.set(data); this.loading.set(false); },
      error: () => this.loading.set(false)
    });
  }

  onDispatch(id: number) {
    this.deliveries.update(list =>
      list.map(d => d.id === id ? { ...d, status: 'DISPATCHED' } : d)
    );
  }
}

sections.heading: input() and output() — signal-based component IO (Angular 17.1+)
sections.content:
// Modern signal-based inputs/outputs
@Component({
  selector: 'app-delivery-card',
  standalone: true,
  template: `
    <div class="card">
      <h3>{{ delivery().driverName }}</h3>
      <p-tag [severity]="statusColor()">{{ delivery().status }}</p-tag>
      <button (click)="dispatched.emit(delivery().id)">Dispatch</button>
    </div>
  `
})
export class DeliveryCardComponent {
  // Signal-based input (replaces @Input())
  delivery = input.required<Delivery>();

  // Signal-based output (replaces @Output() + EventEmitter)
  dispatched = output<number>();

  // Computed from input signal
  statusColor = computed(() => {
    switch (this.delivery().status) {
      case 'DELIVERED': return 'success';
      case 'DELAYED': return 'danger';
      default: return 'info';
    }
  });
}
Benefits over decorators: • Type-safe, no undefined initial state with input.required() • Can use computed() directly from inputs • Better tree-shaking and AOT optimization

sections.heading: Signals vs RxJS — when to use which
sections.content:
Use Signals whenUse RxJS when
Synchronous state (counters, flags, lists)HTTP calls (return Observable)
Derived/computed valuesWebSocket streams
Component local stateComplex async transformations (debounce, retry, combine)
Simple parent→child data flowEvent streams (fromEvent, interval)
Replacing BehaviorSubject for stateWhen you need operators (switchMap, mergeMap)
Hybrid pattern (recommended): 
// Service: RxJS for async, expose as signal
@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class DeliveryStore {
  private http = inject(HttpClient);

  // Internal RxJS (HTTP is inherently async)
  private deliveries$ = this.http.get<Delivery[]>('/api/deliveries');

  // Expose as signal (components consume synchronously)
  deliveries = toSignal(this.deliveries$, { initialValue: [] });

  // Computed
  activeCount = computed(() => this.deliveries().filter(d => d.active).length);
}

sections.heading: Two form approaches in Angular
sections.content:
Angular provides two form systems:
Template-drivenReactive forms
Logic in template (ngModel)Logic in TypeScript (FormControl)
Simple forms, rapid prototypingComplex forms, dynamic fields
Less testableHighly testable (pure TS)
Like JSF managed bean bindingLike Spring @Valid with ConstraintValidator
TRAB preference: Reactive Forms for anything beyond a search box.

sections.heading: Reactive Forms — full control
sections.content:
@Component({
  standalone: true,
  imports: [ReactiveFormsModule, InputTextModule, ButtonModule],
  template: `
    <form [formGroup]="deliveryForm" (ngSubmit)="onSubmit()">
      <div>
        <label for="driver">Driver</label>
        <input pInputText id="driver" formControlName="driverName" />
        @if (f['driverName'].errors?.['required'] && f['driverName'].touched) {
          <small class="p-error">Driver is required</small>
        }
      </div>

      <div>
        <label for="stops">Number of stops</label>
        <input pInputText id="stops" type="number" formControlName="stops" />
        @if (f['stops'].errors?.['min']) {
          <small class="p-error">At least 1 stop required</small>
        }
      </div>

      <button pButton type="submit" [disabled]="deliveryForm.invalid">Create</button>
    </form>
  `
})
export class CreateDeliveryComponent {
  private fb = inject(FormBuilder);

  deliveryForm = this.fb.group({
    driverName: ['', [Validators.required, Validators.minLength(2)]],
    stops: [1, [Validators.required, Validators.min(1), Validators.max(50)]],
    scheduledAt: ['', Validators.required],
    priority: ['NORMAL']
  });

  get f() { return this.deliveryForm.controls; }

  onSubmit() {
    if (this.deliveryForm.valid) {
      const dto = this.deliveryForm.getRawValue();
      // Send to API
    }
  }
}

sections.heading: Custom validators — like Bean Validation
sections.content:
// Custom validator (like @ConstraintValidator in Java)
export function futureDate(): ValidatorFn {
  return (control: AbstractControl): ValidationErrors | null => {
    const date = new Date(control.value);
    return date > new Date() ? null : { futureDate: true };
  };
}

// Async validator (like checking uniqueness against API)
export function uniqueDriver(service: DriverService): AsyncValidatorFn {
  return (control: AbstractControl): Observable<ValidationErrors | null> => {
    return service.exists(control.value).pipe(
      map(exists => exists ? { driverTaken: true } : null),
      catchError(() => of(null))
    );
  };
}

// Usage:
driverName: ['', [Validators.required], [uniqueDriver(this.driverService)]]
Parallel to Java: • Validators.required → @NotNull • Validators.min(1) → @Min(1) • Validators.pattern() → @Pattern • Custom ValidatorFn → ConstraintValidator<A, T>

sections.heading: Testing stack
Component testing
sections.content:
describe('DeliveryCardComponent', () => {
  it('should display driver name', () => {
    const fixture = TestBed.createComponent(DeliveryCardComponent);
    const component = fixture.componentInstance;

    // Set signal input
    fixture.componentRef.setInput('delivery', {
      id: 1, status: 'PLANNED', driverName: 'Hans', scheduledAt: '2024-01-15'
    });

    fixture.detectChanges();

    const el = fixture.nativeElement;
    expect(el.querySelector('h3').textContent).toContain('Hans');
  });

  it('should emit dispatched event on button click', () => {
    const fixture = TestBed.createComponent(DeliveryCardComponent);
    fixture.componentRef.setInput('delivery', {
      id: 42, status: 'PLANNED', driverName: 'Hans', scheduledAt: '2024-01-15'
    });
    fixture.detectChanges();

    let emittedId: number | undefined;
    fixture.componentInstance.dispatched.subscribe(id => emittedId = id);

    fixture.nativeElement.querySelector('button').click();
    expect(emittedId).toBe(42);
  });
});
Testing signals: Call the signal function directly — expect(component.totalActive()).toBe(3).

sections.heading: Testing patterns (pyramid)
sections.content:
LevelWhatTool%
UnitServices, pipes, pure logicJasmine + TestBed60%
IntegrationComponents + template + DITestBed + fixture30%
E2EFull app flowsPlaywright/Cypress10%
What to test in Angular: • Services: business logic, HTTP calls (mock with HttpTestingController) • Components: inputs/outputs, template rendering, user interactions • Pipes: pure transform logic (easiest to test) • Guards: navigation logic What NOT to test: • Angular framework internals (routing works, DI works) • Third-party libraries (PrimeNG renders correctly) • Trivial getters/setters with no logic
